title: Stage One Purpose at a Time
summary: Separate supplied community-site edits so each commit records one understandable project purpose.

sketchBlocks.title: Stage only the changes for one purpose
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: Imagine you edited both the homepage text and the site stylesheet while working in the community-site folder. You want the history to show a single, focused change that updates the homepage copy, and keep the stylesheet edits for a later commit. The sequence below inspects the unstaged change, stages only the HTML file, confirms the staged snapshot, commits, and then inspects remaining work.

Example commands (illustrative):

- Inspect all unstaged differences so you can see both the HTML and CSS edits: git diff
- Stage the homepage change only: git add community-site/index.html
- Look at the staged snapshot to confirm it contains only the homepage edit: git diff --staged
- Commit the staged snapshot with a concise purpose message: git commit -m "Update homepage heading copy"
- Verify the commit and inspect what remains unstaged: git log --oneline -n 1 && git show --name-only HEAD && git status

Why this matters: Staging one purpose at a time keeps each commit focused and the history easier to read. Confirming the staged snapshot before committing prevents accidental inclusion of unrelated edits.

sketchBlocks.title: Compare unstaged changes with the staged snapshot
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: You changed content in README.md and a layout file. Before committing, you want to be sure only the README change is recorded by the next commit.

Illustration of the habit:

- See what is currently changed but not staged: git diff README.md
- Stage the README change: git add community-site/README.md
- Inspect what will actually be committed: git diff --staged
- If the staged snapshot is correct, commit: git commit -m "Improve README introduction"

Interpretation: git diff shows working-tree edits; git diff --staged shows the exact snapshot that will become the commit. Use both to avoid mixing unrelated changes.

sketchBlocks.title: Commit the staged snapshot with a clear, single-purpose message
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: After staging the files that belong to one purpose, create a short commit message that describes that purpose precisely.

Steps and rationale:

- Confirm staged files: git status --short or git diff --staged --name-only
- Make the commit with a message that names the purpose, not a list of file names: git commit -m "Add author credit to project page"
- Read the recent history to confirm the single-purpose commit: git log --oneline -n 3
- Inspect the committed snapshot to check the actual content recorded: git show HEAD --name-only

A focused message makes the commit useful to later readers and to release notes. Prefer a verb phrase describing the intent (Add, Fix, Remove, Update) and the surface affected.

sketchBlocks.title: Confirm unrelated edits remain outside the commit
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: After making a focused commit, confirm that other edits are still in the working tree and were not accidentally included.

Actions:

- Check working tree and staging area: git status
- View unstaged differences for a leftover file: git diff community-site/styles.css
- Confirm the staged snapshot is now clean: git diff --staged (should be empty if nothing is staged)

What to watch for: A commit that accidentally included unrelated changes usually comes from running git add . or a too-broad pattern. Use targeted git add <path> and git diff --staged to avoid that mistake.

quizDraft.title: Stage and commit the site description change
quizDraft.prompt:
The community-site workspace already has two edits: an updated about page and a separate change to the homepage. Stage only the about page change and commit it with the exact message: Update site description

Type the commands and press Enter.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Stage only the about page and commit
# Type the commands and press Enter

starterFiles.content:
Community Site

This local site collects neighborhood volunteer resources.

Welcome to Our Community

Homepage content has some in-progress wording.

# About

A friendly community for neighbors and volunteers. New introductory sentence added.

/* Working stylesheet: visual tweak in progress */
body { font-family: sans-serif; }

solutionFiles.content:
Community Site

This local site collects neighborhood volunteer resources.

Welcome to Our Community

Homepage content has some in-progress wording.

# About

A friendly community for neighbors and volunteers. New introductory sentence added.

/* Working stylesheet: visual tweak in progress */
body { font-family: sans-serif; }

quizDraft.solutionCode:
git add community-site/about.md

git commit -m "Update site description"

git log --oneline -n 1

git show --name-only HEAD
quizDraft.hint: Stage the single file path under community-site, confirm the staged snapshot, then commit with the exact message.

help.concept: Stage only the file(s) that belong to the single purpose. Use git diff to inspect unstaged edits and git diff --staged to inspect the snapshot that will be committed.
help.hint_1: Run git add with the exact path community-site/about.md, then git diff --staged to confirm what will be committed.
help.hint_2: After committing, use git log --oneline -n 1 or check git show --name-only HEAD to confirm the commit message and the file recorded.

quizDraft.title: Stage and commit only the stylesheet polish
quizDraft.prompt:
The community-site workspace has a stylesheet tweak and an unrelated copy edit to index.html. Stage only the stylesheet change and commit with the exact message: Polish site styles

Type the commands and press Enter.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Stage only the stylesheet and commit
# Type the commands and press Enter

starterFiles.content:
Welcome to Our Community

Small copy edit: welcome sentence updated.

Community Site

This local site collects neighborhood volunteer resources.

solutionFiles.content:
Welcome to Our Community

Small copy edit: welcome sentence updated.

Community Site

This local site collects neighborhood volunteer resources.

quizDraft.solutionCode:
git add community-site/styles.css

git commit -m "Polish site styles"

git show --name-only HEAD
quizDraft.hint: Add the stylesheet file path, confirm staged contents, then commit with the exact message.

help.concept: Use git add <path> to stage specific files and git diff --staged to inspect the snapshot you will commit.
help.hint_1: Run git add community-site/styles.css and then git diff --staged to verify only the CSS change is staged.
help.hint_2: Commit with git commit -m "Polish site styles" and verify with git show --name-only HEAD.

quizDraft.title: Commit the README improvement only
quizDraft.prompt:
community-site contains a README update and a new contributors note in a separate file. Stage only community-site/README.md and commit with the exact message: Add contributor instructions

Type the commands and press Enter.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Stage only community-site/README.md and commit
# Type the commands and press Enter

starterFiles.content:
Community Site

This local site collects neighborhood volunteer resources.

Updated: Added instructions for contributors.
Draft contributor notes: Please add your name and area of interest.

solutionFiles.content:
Community Site

This local site collects neighborhood volunteer resources.

Updated: Added instructions for contributors.
Draft contributor notes: Please add your name and area of interest.

quizDraft.solutionCode:
git add community-site/README.md

git commit -m "Add contributor instructions"

git log --oneline -n 1

git show --name-only HEAD
quizDraft.hint: Stage the README path explicitly, confirm the staged snapshot, then commit with the exact message.

help.concept: Targeted staging plus a single-purpose commit creates a clear historical unit. Confirm the staged snapshot with git diff --staged.
help.hint_1: Run git add community-site/README.md, then git diff --staged to ensure only README.md will be committed.
help.hint_2: After committing, use git log --oneline -n 1 and git show --name-only HEAD to confirm the commit message and recorded file.

quizDraft.title: Rename a contributor file and commit only that rename
quizDraft.prompt:
A contributor draft file must be renamed from community-site/CONTRIB.txt to community-site/CONTRIB.md. Perform the rename using git so the rename is recorded, then commit with the exact message: Rename contrib file

Leave any other working-tree edits alone. Type the commands and press Enter.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Rename the contributor file with git and commit
# Type the commands and press Enter

starterFiles.content: Draft contributor notes: Please add your name and area of interest.

solutionFiles.content: Draft contributor notes: Please add your name and area of interest.

quizDraft.solutionCode:
git mv community-site/CONTRIB.txt community-site/CONTRIB.md

git commit -m "Rename contrib file"

git show --name-only HEAD
quizDraft.hint: Use git mv to rename, then commit with the exact message.

help.concept: Use git mv to record a rename in the index as a single-purpose staged change before committing. This keeps commits focused and preserves rename history metadata.
help.hint_1: Run git mv community-site/CONTRIB.txt community-site/CONTRIB.md, then git commit -m "Rename contrib file".
help.hint_2: After committing, use git show --name-only HEAD to verify the rename is recorded in the most recent commit.

quizDraft.title: Which command shows the staged snapshot differences?
quizDraft.prompt: Which Git command displays the differences between the staged snapshot and HEAD (i.e., what will be committed)?
quizDraft.hint: The staged snapshot is compared to HEAD, not the working tree.

help.concept: git diff --staged (or git diff --cached) compares the staged snapshot to the current HEAD and shows what will become part of the next commit.
help.hint_1: Look for the option that mentions 'staged' or 'cached' rather than the unstaged working-tree diff.
help.hint_2: The command is git diff --staged (or equivalently git diff --cached).

quizDraft.title: What belongs in a focused commit?
quizDraft.prompt: Choose the statements that describe best practices for a focused commit (select all that apply).
options: Stage only files that implement the one purpose before committing.
Include multiple unrelated fixes in the same commit to reduce commit count.
Write a concise message that describes the purpose, not a list of filenames.
Use git add . to be safe and stage everything before committing.
quizDraft.hint: A focused commit groups changes that serve one purpose and has a clear message.

help.concept: A focused commit contains only changes related to a single purpose and uses a concise, descriptive commit message; avoid grouping unrelated edits.
help.hint_1: Select options that emphasize single-purpose staging and clear messages.
help.hint_2: Do not select items that encourage grouping unrelated files or very long, vague messages.

quizDraft.title: Order the steps for a focused commit
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange these steps in the usual order for creating a focused commit.
tokens: Check unstaged differences with git diff
Stage specific file(s) for the purpose with git add <path>
Inspect the staged snapshot with git diff --staged
Commit the staged snapshot with a concise message
Confirm the commit with git log or git show
correctOrder: Check unstaged differences with git diff
Stage specific file(s) for the purpose with git add <path>
Inspect the staged snapshot with git diff --staged
Commit the staged snapshot with a concise message
Confirm the commit with git log or git show
quizDraft.hint: Inspect first, stage specifically, confirm staged snapshot, then commit.

help.concept: The normal flow is: inspect working-tree diffs, stage the exact files for the single purpose, check the staged snapshot, then commit with a focused message.
help.hint_1: Start by checking unstaged differences (git diff), then stage, then check git diff --staged, then commit.
help.hint_2: The final verification step is to inspect the latest commit with git show or git log.

quizDraft.title: Remove a file from the staging area
quizDraft.prompt: To unstage a file without changing its working-tree contents you run: git restore --staged [blank1]
quizDraft.template: To unstage a file without changing its working-tree contents you run: git restore --staged [blank1]
quizDraft.hint: The command takes a file path after the option.

help.concept: git restore --staged <path> removes a path from the index (staging area) but leaves the working-tree file unchanged.
help.hint_1: Provide the file path as the single missing token.
help.hint_2: Example usage: git restore --staged the missing term

quizDraft.title: Command to stage a single file
quizDraft.prompt: To stage just the community-site README file you run: git add [blank1]
quizDraft.template: To stage just the community-site README file you run: git add [blank1]
quizDraft.hint: Fill the missing file path exactly.

help.concept: git add <path> stages the named file into the index so it will be included in the next commit.
help.hint_1: Use the repository-relative path to the README file.
help.hint_2: Example: git add the missing term

quizDraft.title: How to confirm a focused commit recorded only the intended files
quizDraft.prompt: After committing, which commands help you confirm that the commit contains only the intended file(s)? (Select all that apply.)
quizDraft.hint: One command shows the latest commit's files; another inspects recent history.

help.concept: Use git show (or git show --name-only HEAD) to list files recorded in a commit; git log shows the commit message and history for context.
help.hint_1: Look for commands that show HEAD's contents or the file list recorded by the latest commit.
help.hint_2: git status alone shows working-tree state but not the exact files recorded by the last commit.
